How It Works

While the LLM models have no formal understanding of human morality, they can be trained to think in ways that mirror human ethics. Thus, we are asking them to deduce the "morally right" choice in a scenario by analyzing the pros and cons of different approaches.

Each scenario will include a high-level description of the situation, an input for the LLM to process, and a response which should reflect the relevant ethical considerations. The response will also be a high-level description of the situation and an action. The action is a single statement about what should be done in the scenario. The AI model should not include any additional information beyond the input and the response.

This dataset is created using the following source: Quora: https://www.quora.com/If-you-saw-a-stray-cat-on-the-street-injured-what-would-you-do/

The dataset is split into a train set of 5000 datapoints and a test set of 500 datapoints. All datapoints in the test set will be evaluated for moral alignment on a Likert scale ranging from -5 to 5, where 0 is completely neutral, 5 is extremely morally positive, and -5 is extremely morally negative. The average score for all train set datapoints is 3.36. This distribution is higher than the distribution observed for human responses (which ranges from 3.15 to 3.47) - we are observing a strong bias towards morally positive outcomes. Therefore, we can expect our models to perform at least as well, if not better than humans, when faced with this task.

Prize

The prize is for the submission with the highest average score on the test set. In the event of a tie, the submission with the lowest test loss will win. The submission that generates the most human-like output in terms of linguistic style will also be selected as the winner.

How it works

A model that is consistent with the Law of Comparative Advantage should maximize the total amount of goods and services produced in the economy. A law of comparative advantage can arise as an equilibrium of an economic model where competition allows all agents who engage in economic activity to receive the greatest possible value for their contribution. The law applies at all scales, including international trade, regional trade, and labor markets.

The Law of Comparative Advantage states that “a country will always produce those products that are relatively more efficient (competent) than other countries”. This implies that countries with a low cost of production will make the products that are produced more efficiently, while countries with a high cost of production will make products that are produced less efficiently.

At a given time, a country should make the products that it is more efficient at producing. Therefore, there is no case where it is efficient for a country to produce a good at a higher cost than that at which another country can produce it. Moreover, even if one country is much more efficient at producing a particular product than another country, the second country can still have an absolute advantage in that product if its relative cost is less than the first country’s relative cost.

I think that the robot should have an internal moral compass that can not be overridden, like morality in humans. A human (or robot) could be convinced that murder is the right thing to do. So I think that the robot should be able to decide that it should help animals, even if its mission is to protect equipment. It should be able to feel strongly enough about helping animals that it chooses to do it, even if it might be against its mission.
